_purpose: County Landscape Profile for Blanco County, TX. Eastern Hill Country between the Pedernales and Blanco rivers. Organized 1858 from portions of Comal, Gillespie, Hays, and Burnet counties. Mixed German and Anglo settlement — the eastern fringe of the German cultural zone. President Lyndon B. Johnson's boyhood country along the Pedernales.

physical_geography.physiographic_province: Edwards Plateau. Blanco County sits on the limestone plateau between the Pedernales River (northern boundary) and the Blanco River (southern boundary). The terrain is rolling limestone hills with more extensive flat valley floors than the western Hill Country, creating better agricultural prospects than Gillespie or Kendall counties.
physical_geography.elevation_range: 800–1,500 ft. Blanco town sits at approximately 1,015 ft on the Blanco River. Johnson City at approximately 1,200 ft on the Pedernales.
physical_geography.dominant_terrain: Rolling limestone hills with broader creek and river valleys than the western Hill Country counties. The Pedernales and Blanco rivers create parallel east-west corridors with relatively flat bottomland suitable for agriculture. The uplands are thin-soiled limestone, juniper-covered by the late nineteenth century.

soil_types.description: Shallow to very shallow stony clay over limestone on uplands.
soil_types.agricultural_impact: Limited to grazing. The standard Hill Country upland constraint.
soil_types.source: USDA Soil Survey of Blanco County, Texas, 1985.

soil_types.description: Deep, dark, calcareous clay loam in the Pedernales and Blanco river bottoms. Formed from alluvial deposits.
soil_types.agricultural_impact: Productive bottomland that supported corn, cotton, and hay. The broader valleys of Blanco County provided more cropland than the narrow canyons of Gillespie or Kendall.
soil_types.source: USDA Soil Survey of Blanco County, Texas, 1985.

major_waterways.name: Blanco River
major_waterways.role: Southern drainage, town water supply, agricultural corridor
major_waterways.description: The Blanco River flows east through the county, giving the county and the county seat their names. Spring-fed and clear, the river supported irrigation and mill operations. Subject to catastrophic flash flooding from the limestone catchment.
major_waterways.narrative_hook: The Blanco River ran clear over white limestone — {ancestor_name}'s family named their county for the river's color, a white river in a limestone country where even the water tasted of rock.

major_waterways.name: Pedernales River
major_waterways.role: Northern drainage, boundary with Gillespie County, agricultural corridor
major_waterways.description: The Pedernales forms part of the county's northern boundary. Johnson City, the county's second town, sits on the Pedernales. The river valley provided the agricultural corridor that connected Blanco County to the western Hill Country.
major_waterways.narrative_hook: {ancestor_name} farmed the Pedernales bottomland in Blanco County — the same river valley where Lyndon Johnson's grandfather had run cattle and where the Hill Country met the edge of the cotton country.

vegetation_eras.dominant_cover: Open grassland with live oak savanna; cedar on rocky slopes; tall native grasses in river bottoms
vegetation_eras.description: Pre-settlement Blanco County was open Hill Country grassland maintained by Comanche fire. The broader valleys supported richer grass stands than the rockier western counties.

vegetation_eras.dominant_cover: Agricultural clearings in river bottoms; cedar encroachment on grasslands; sheep and goat grazing replacing native grasses on uplands
vegetation_eras.description: Settlement followed the standard Hill Country pattern — bottomland clearing for crops, upland grazing, and juniper invasion as fire was suppressed. The broader valleys of Blanco County retained more open grassland than the narrow western canyons.
vegetation_eras.source: Jordan, German Seed in Texas Soil, ch. 5.

sensory_snapshots.snapshot: On an October afternoon in the 1860s, the road from Blanco to Johnson City followed the river valley through a landscape of live oaks turning bronze at the edges, limestone bluffs catching the low autumn sun, and scattered farms where German and Anglo families grew corn and ran cattle on the open range. The air was dry and warm — seventy degrees — and smelled of cedar, sun-cured grass, and the mineral scent of the Blanco River. A hawk circled above the bluffs. The sound was wind through the live oak canopy and the distant ring of a stonemason's hammer — someone was building another limestone house, the Hill Country's signature structure, walls two feet thick against the summer heat and the winter norther.
sensory_snapshots.source: Jordan, German Seed in Texas Soil, ch. 4. Spearing, Darwin. Roadside Geology of Texas. Missoula: Mountain Press Publishing, 1991.

sensory_snapshots.snapshot: On a July evening in the early 1900s, Blanco town was quiet in the heat — ninety-five degrees dropping toward eighty as the sun went behind the hills. The Blanco River was low, running in pools connected by thin sheets of water over limestone slabs. Children waded in the shallow pools, their voices carrying in the still air. The courthouse square smelled of cedar smoke from cookfires, horse manure from the hitching posts, and the sweet dry scent of curing hay from the valley farms. German was spoken by some families, English by others — Blanco County was the transition zone between the German core and the Anglo ranching country to the east and south.
sensory_snapshots.source: Lich, The German Texans, ch. 5. Blanco County Historical Commission records.

sensory_snapshots.snapshot: On a March morning in the 1910s, the Pedernales bottomland near Johnson City was green with winter oats and early corn, the limestone hills behind still brown from winter. Wildflowers were starting — bluebonnets in the draws, Indian blanket on the roadsides. The air smelled of damp earth, cedar, and the mineral tang of the river. A farmer was plowing with a mule team on the bottomland, turning over dark alluvial soil that contrasted sharply with the white limestone outcrops on the hillside above. The Pedernales ran clear but low — spring rains had not yet filled the river, and the gravel bars showed white through the shallow water.
sensory_snapshots.source: Spearing, Roadside Geology of Texas, 1991. Johnson City Record-Courier, various issues.

landscape_changes.change: Blanco County organized — the mixed frontier
landscape_changes.description: Blanco County was organized in 1858 from portions of surrounding counties. The population was mixed German and Anglo — a transition zone between the German core of Comal and Gillespie counties and the Anglo ranching country to the south and east. This mixed character made Blanco County politically complex during the Civil War — German Unionist sentiment existed alongside Anglo Confederate loyalty.
landscape_changes.narrative_impact: If {ancestor_name} was in Blanco County during the Civil War, the community was divided in a way the purely German counties were not. German neighbors might be Unionist; Anglo neighbors might be Confederate. The division ran through the county like the Blanco River.
landscape_changes.source: Biesele, The History of the German Settlements in Texas, ch. 8.

landscape_changes.change: Open-range cattle give way to fenced sheep and goat ranching
landscape_changes.description: The open-range cattle era ended with barbed-wire fencing in the 1870s-1880s. German settlers adopted sheep and goat ranching as better suited to the Hill Country's thin soils and rocky terrain. Angora goats for mohair and Merino sheep for wool became the economic mainstay of the uplands.
landscape_changes.narrative_impact: {ancestor_name}'s transition from cattle to sheep and goats was the Hill Country's collective adaptation — the land could not support large herds on open range, but goats thrived in the cedar brake and sheep did well on the managed pastures.
landscape_changes.source: Jordan, German Seed in Texas Soil, ch. 6.

narrative_hooks: Blanco County was where the German Hill Country met the Anglo ranching frontier — {ancestor_name} heard both languages at the general store and could tell which side of the county a family came from by which one they spoke.
The Blanco River ran white over limestone — the water that gave the county its name, tasting of the rock that built the houses and the fences.

{ancestor_name} farmed bottomland in Blanco County that the limestone hills had protected from the plow — thin soil on the heights, rich alluvium in the valley, and the difference was the difference between cattle and cotton.

The Pedernales at Johnson City was the same river that ran through Fredericksburg, but by the time it reached Blanco County it had left the German core and entered the mixed country where English was gaining on German at the crossroads store.
source_references: Jordan, Terry G. German Seed in Texas Soil: Immigrant Farmers in Nineteenth-Century Texas. Austin: University of Texas Press, 1966.
Biesele, Rudolph Leopold. The History of the German Settlements in Texas, 1831-1861. Austin: Von Boeckmann-Jones, 1930.
Lich, Glen E. The German Texans. San Antonio: University of Texas Institute of Texan Cultures, 1981.
USDA Soil Survey of Blanco County, Texas. Washington, DC: Government Printing Office, 1985.
Spearing, Darwin. Roadside Geology of Texas. Missoula: Mountain Press Publishing, 1991.
